sources.notes: Statement and constraints: for each query vertex, find the nearest edge that lies on every path from 1 to n.
Official solution uses bridges on the 1-to-n DFS path and multi-source BFS.
Cross-check via edge-biconnected components, bridge tree path, and BFS from required bridge endpoints.

annotation.summary: In a connected undirected simple graph, answer for each vertex the nearest edge that every path from 1 to n must use, breaking ties by smallest edge index.
annotation.constraints: t <= 10000, sum n,m,q <= 200000; requires linear or near-linear preprocessing per test case.
annotation.core_idea: An edge is used by every 1-to-n path exactly when it is a bridge separating 1 and n, equivalently a bridge on the 1-to-n path in the bridge-block tree. Find those required bridges by lowlink DFS with path marking, or by compressing edge-biconnected components and taking the bridge-tree path. Then run a multi-source BFS from all endpoints of required bridges, carrying the bridge index and distance; equal-distance ties keep the smallest index.
annotation.complexity: O(n + m + q) per test case
tricks: Required edges are exactly separating bridges for terminals 1 and n.
Mark only bridges on the 1-to-n bridge-tree path.
Multi-source BFS answers nearest required edge for all query vertices at once.

solution_variants.name: lowlink-path-bfs
solution_variants.summary: Run DFS from 1, compute lowlink bridges while marking tree edges on the DFS route to n, initialize all required bridge endpoints, then multi-source BFS.

solution_variants.name: bridge-block-tree
solution_variants.summary: Compress 2-edge-connected components, find the path between components of 1 and n in the bridge tree, then BFS from endpoints of bridges on that path.

tags.evidence: The mandatory lanes are precisely bridges whose removal separates vertex 1 from vertex n.
All query answers are obtained by a multi-source BFS from endpoints of the mandatory bridges.
Lowlink DFS is used to compute bridges and mark the 1-to-n DFS path.
The bridge-block tree variant relies on 2-edge-connected components and component compression.
After bridge compression, the relevant mandatory edges form the unique path between the terminal components in a tree.
